title: CXR — Approach
oneLiner: Systematic interpretation of frontal/lateral chest radiographs; essential for any acute patient; missed findings cause preventable deterioration.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Vignette:** A 68-year-old smoker presents to ED with acute dyspnoea, right-sided pleuritic chest pain, and productive cough of green sputum for three days. Heart rate 110 bpm, temperature 38.2°C, oxygen saturations 89% on room air. Chest exam reveals decreased breath sounds at the right base with bronchial breathing and coarse crackles. Trachea is midline. The junior doctor orders a PA erect CXR.

- **Pleuritic pain + cough + fever + hypoxaemia** → high suspicion of community-acquired pneumonia; CXR will likely show lobar consolidation.
- **Reduced breath sounds and bronchial breathing** → air bronchograms and opacity on the affected lung region.
- **Midline trachea** → no significant mass effect or tension physiology.
- **Smoker + age >65** → increased risk of underlying malignancy; look for additional mass or effusion.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Silent pneumonia** – patient may present with confusion and tachypnoea without cough or fever; CXR shows subtle opacity (especially in elderly).
- **The "normal" early CXR** – in early pneumonia, especially interstitial, the CXR can be normal for 12–24 hours; repeat if strong clinical suspicion.
- **Post-obstructive pneumonia** – from an endobronchial tumour; CXR may show distal consolidation with no air bronchogram and secondary signs like hilar mass or volume loss.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** A **Chest X-ray (CXR) approach** is a structured, systematic method to evaluate the frontal (and when available, lateral) chest radiograph for technical quality, anatomy, and all abnormalities — from lungs and pleura to heart, mediastinum, bones, and soft tissues.

**Why it matters:**  
- CXR is the most frequently requested imaging study — up to 5 million performed annually in the UK alone.  
- An unstructured review misses 20–30% of significant findings (e.g., subtle pneumothorax, small effusion, hilar mass).  
- Junior doctors are expected to interpret CXRs immediately in acute settings; error leads to delayed treatment for conditions like tension pneumothorax, pericardial effusion, or aortic dissection.  
- Common pitfalls include focusing only on the lungs, misidentifying artefacts (e.g., ECG leads, buttons, nipples), and failing to note tube/line positions.  
- The approach must be **reproducible, complete, and fast** — the ABCDE method is standard.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **X-ray attenuation:** Four basic densities on a CXR — air (black), fat (grey), soft tissue/water (white), bone (whiter), metal (brightest). The lung appears mostly black because it is ~80% air. Any increase in density (opacity) or decrease (lucency) means pathology.

- **Silhouette sign:** Loss of the normal border between two structures of same radiodensity. Example: a right middle lobe pneumonia effaces the right heart border (because the middle lobe is anterior and adjacent to the heart). A lower lobe pneumonia does not — it silhouettes the diaphragm.

- **Consolidation (air-space opacification):** Alveoli filled with exudate/pus/blood/water → X-rays are absorbed more → white area. **Air bronchogram** — black branching air-filled bronchi visible within the white consolidation — occurs because bronchi remain patent while surrounding alveoli are filled. This is the single most reliable sign of air-space disease (pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, etc.) and essentially rules out atelectasis (where bronchi also collapse).

- **Atelectasis:** Volume loss from alveolar collapse. The lung appears opaque but with **no air bronchogram** (collapsed bronchi); instead, you see displacement of fissures, hila, or mediastinum toward the collapsed side.

- **Pleural effusion:** Fluid in the pleural space layers posteriorly and inferiorly. On an erect PA film, the dependent costophrenic angle blunts initially; larger effusions produce a **meniscus sign** (concave upward border). Supine films cause fluid to layer posteriorly, making the hemithorax appear uniformly hazy.

- **Pneumothorax:** Air in the pleural cavity separates visceral from parietal pleura. The visceral pleura becomes visible as a **thin, white, hairline** (pleural line) with **no lung markings beyond it** (hyperlucent area). In tension, the mediastinum shifts away, and the hemidiaphragm is depressed.

- **Pulmonary oedema:** Interstitial oedema first → Kerley B lines (short horizontal lines at costophrenic angles) and peribronchial cuffing; then alveolar oedema → bilateral central "bat wing" opacities with air bronchograms.

- **Mass:** A tumour (primary or metastatic) appears as a rounded opacity with varying borders. Benign masses often have smooth, well-defined borders; malignant masses may have spiculated borders (corona radiata) or a feeding vessel sign.

- **Mediastinal widening:** May be from aortic dissection (irregular aortic knuckle), lymphadenopathy (bilateral hilar masses – typically sarcoid, TB, lymphoma), or thymoma.

- **Bone lesions:** Rib fractures (especially in trauma) may be subtle on CXR; look for cortical disruption, step-off, or displacement. Osteolytic metastases appear as lucent defects.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**  
- The vignette with consolidation, bronchial breath sounds, and fever is explained by air bronchograms and alveolar filling – pattern of pneumonia.  
- Midline trachea in tension pneumothorax would be a critical shift; absence of shift suggests simple pneumothorax.  
- A silent pneumonia in the elderly: immunosenescence reduces radiographic density, so the opacity may be faint; look for subtle blunting of angle or loss of sharp cardiac border.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Causes | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Infectious** | Community-acquired pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, viruses), hospital-acquired (MRSA, Gram-negatives), tuberculosis, fungal (immunocompromised) | Fever, cough, consolidation with air bronchogram |
| **Cardiogenic** | Acute pulmonary oedema (LVF, fluid overload) | Bilateral central opacity, Kerley B lines, cardiomegaly, effusions |
| **Neoplastic** | Primary lung cancer (SCC, adenocarcinoma), metastasis | Solitary pulmonary nodule (SPN), hilar mass, effusion, no air bronchogram |
| **Traumatic** | Rib fracture, pneumothorax, haemothorax, contusion | History of trauma, rib tenderness, pleural line, opacification from contusion |
| **Autoimmune/Inflammatory** | Sarcoidosis, connective tissue disease (rheumatoid, SLE), hypersensitivity pneumonitis | Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, interstitial pattern, restrictive changes |
| **Idiopathic** | Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), cryptogenic organising pneumonia (COP) | Basal-predominant reticular pattern, honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis |
| **Iatrogenic** | Line misplacement (central line in artery, NG tube in airway), post-procedural pneumothorax, aspiration | Clinical context: after procedure, intubation, or feeding |
| **Vascular** | Pulmonary embolism (rarely visible – only if infarct) | Hampton's hump (wedge-shaped pleural-based opacity), Westermark's sign (oligaemia) – rarely seen |
| **Other** | Foreign body aspiration, atelectasis (post-op, mucus plug), pleural effusion (para-pneumonic, malignant) | Specific history or post-operative state |

**Most common cause overall:** Infection (community-acquired pneumonia) accounts for the vast majority of focal opacities in ambulatory patients. For bilateral diffuse opacities, pulmonary oedema is most common in hospitalised patients.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**The CXR itself is the primary investigation.** The key is a systematic interpretation.

**Systematic ABCDE approach (mnemonic):**

- **A – Airways:** Trace the trachea (midline?), carina (saddle angle <90°?), main bronchi. Look for diver or NG tube misplacement.
- **B – Breathing / Lungs:** Check for:
  - **Pneumothorax** – pleural line, deep sulcus sign (supine), lack of lung markings.
  - **Consolidation** – air bronchogram, opacity, no volume loss.
  - **Atelectasis** – volume loss, no air bronchogram.
  - **Effusion** – blunted costophrenic angle, meniscus.
  - **Diffuse interstitial pattern** – reticular, nodular, or ground-glass.
- **C – Cardiac / Mediastinum:** Heart size (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 abnormal), mediastinal width (normal <8 cm at the aortic arch), aortic knuckle, hilar position (left hilum usually higher). Check for water bottle heart (pericardial effusion), widened mediastinum (dissection/aneurysm).
- **D – Diaphragm:** Right hemi 1–2 cm higher than left. Flattening suggests hyperinflation (COPD). Look for air under diaphragm (free gas – perforation).
- **E – Everything else:** Bones (ribs, clavicles, spine – fracture/destruction), soft tissues (subcutaneous emphysema), shoulders, upper abdomen (gastric bubble, colonic ileus).

**Additional views/techniques:**
- **Lateral CXR** – clarifies retrocardiac, retrosternal areas, and confirms effusions or masses seen on frontal.
- **Decubitus film** – distinguishes free-flowing (layers) from loculated effusion.
- **Expiration view** – increases sensitivity for small pneumothorax (look for visceral pleural line).
- **CT chest** – gold standard when CXR equivocal for malignancy, PE, ILD, or complex infection.

**Single finding that often confirms:**
- **Air bronchogram** → essentially diagnostic of air-space consolidation (pneumonia/oedema) and excludes atelectasis.
- **Visceral pleural line** without lung markings → confirms pneumothorax.
- **Meniscus sign and blunted CP angle** → confirms pleural effusion of at least 200 mL.

**Rules out (with high sensitivity if negative):**
- A completely normal CXR in a patient with pleuritic pain and dyspnoea makes significant pleural or parenchymal disease unlikely; consider PE or musculoskeletal cause.
- In trauma, a normal CXR does not exclude aortic injury – CT required.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
Management is based on the CXR finding. The table below summarises immediate, ward, and long-term actions.

| Finding | Immediate (first hour) | Ward (24–72h) | Long-term | What NOT to do |
|---------|------------------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Tension pneumothorax** | Immediate needle decompression (14G, 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line or 5th space midaxillary) → large-bore chest drain (28–36Fr, 5th intercostal space midaxillary). | – | Follow-up CXR after drain removal; refer to respiratory if recurrent spontaneous PTx. | Wait for CXR confirmation if clinical suspicion high (distended neck veins, hypotension, absent breath sounds) – decompress first. |
| **Simple pneumothorax** | If >2cm (from pleural line to chest wall at hilum) or symptomatic, insert chest drain (12–14Fr). If <2cm and asymptomatic, observe. | Re-check CXR at 4–6h; if stable size, discharge with advice. | Consider pleurodesis if recurrent. | Do not insert drain for tiny apical pneumothorax <2cm in an asymptomatic patient (risk of conversion to tension). |
| **Large pleural effusion (new)** | Diagnostic thoracentesis (US-guided) – send for LDH, protein, pH, cytology, Gram stain, culture. | If empyema (pH<7.2, glucose<2.2) → chest drain; antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV QDS). | Treat underlying cause: diuretics for heart failure, anti-TB therapy, chemotherapy for malignant. | Do not drain a transudative effusion on appearance alone – check Light's criteria. |
| **Lobar pneumonia with consolidation** | Start antibiotics within 1h: for CAP: amoxicillin 500mg PO TDS (mild) or amoxicillin 1g IV TDS + clarithromycin 500mg IV BD (severe). If healthcare-associated: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV QDS. | Adjust based on cultures; switch to oral when clinically improved. | Check CXR at 6 weeks in smokers or those with risk factors to rule out underlying mass. | Do not give antibiotics for a CXR showing only atelectasis; observe. |
| **Pulmonary oedema (cardiogenic)** | IV furosemide 40–80mg IV (or 5× daily dose if on loop diuretic). Give O2 target 94–98%, consider CPAP if severe. | Optimise heart failure therapy (ACEi, beta-blocker); check echo. | Long-term diuretic dose adjustment, salt restriction, exercise. | Do not fluid bolus (even if hypotensive due to poor LV function); try vasopressor if needed (e.g., dobutamine). |
| **Widened mediastinum (suspected dissection)** | Urgent CT aortogram. BP control: labetalol 20mg IV bolus then infusion 1–2mg/min; target HR 60 bpm and SBP 100–120 mmHg. | If dissection confirmed, surgical or endovascular repair. | Lifelong antihypertensive therapy. | Do not wait for lab results to act; manage immediately. |

**General pitfall:** Never interpret a CXR in isolation from clinical context. Always compare with previous films if available.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
For a **unilateral opacity on CXR**, key differentials: consolidation vs atelectasis vs mass vs effusion.

| Feature | Consolidation (Pneumonia) | Atelectasis | Mass (Tumour) | Pleural Effusion |
|---------|---------------------------|-------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Air bronchogram** | Present | Absent (bronchi also collapse) | Absent (unless secondary pneumonitis) | Absent |
| **Volume loss** | None (or minimal) | Prominent (fissure shift, hilar retraction, mediastinal shift) | None (or if endobronchial, distal atelectasis may be present) | None (but effusion may cause contralateral shift if large) |
| **Borders** | Fleshy, ill-defined, lobar | Concave, sharp at fissures | Well- or ill-defined, rounded | Meniscus sign – concave upward |
| **Silhouette sign** | Yes – e.g., RML pneumonia blurs right heart border | No – atelectasis blurs only if directly adjacent (e.g., LLL atel blurs left hemi-diaphragm) | Usually not – unless it involves adjacent mediastinal structures | May not silhouette unless large; often posterior |
| **Shape** | Lobular or diffuse | Linear, triangular, or wedge | Spherical/oval | Homogeneous, blunting CP angle |
| **Clinical context** | Fever, productive cough, pleuritic pain | Post-operative, pain (mucus plug), shallow breathing | Smoker, weight loss, haemoptysis | Dyspnoea, dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds |
| **Key differentiator** | Air bronchogram + no volume loss = consolidation | Volume loss + no air bronchogram = atelectasis | History + absence of air bronchogram ± spiculated border | Meniscus sign + homogeneous opacity + blunted CP angle |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Missed Finding | How It Presents Late | Key Signs | What To Do |
|----------------|----------------------|-----------|------------|
| **Small pneumothorax missed** | Tension physiology develops (especially on positive pressure ventilation) | Hypotension, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds | Immediate needle decompression → chest drain. |
| **Pneumonia without treatment** | Sepsis, lung abscess, empyema (persistent fever, high WBC, pleural pain) | CXR shows new air-fluid level in consolidation (abscess) or loculated effusion (empyema) | CT chest, drainage of empyema, broaden antibiotics. |
| **Lung cancer missed** | Presentation with SVC obstruction (facial swelling, distended neck veins), haemoptysis, or malignant effusion | Mediastinal widening, hilar mass, or massive effusion | Urgent CT thorax + PET, refer to lung cancer MDT. |
| **Aortic dissection missed** | Rupture – sudden haemodynamic collapse, cardiac tamponade | Widened mediastinum, hypotension, pericardial effusion on CXR | CT aortogram, urgent surgical referral. |
| **Pulmonary oedema missed** | Progressive hypoxia, acute respiratory failure, need for intubation | Bilateral central opacities, PaO2/FiO2 ratio <300 | CPAP, IV diuretics, ICU if NIV fails. |
| **Pericardial effusion (missed on CXR)** | Cardiac tamponade: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins (Beck's triad) | Enlarged cardiac silhouette (water bottle heart) + clear lungs | Urgent echo, pericardiocentesis. |

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: ABCDE**  
- **A** – Airways (trachea, bronchi, tubes)  
- **B** – Breathing (lungs: pneumothorax, consolidation, effusion, atelectasis, interstitial)  
- **C** – Cardiac (heart size, mediastinal width)  
- **D** – Diaphragm (position, free gas)  
- **E** – Everything else (bones, soft tissues, lines)

**Mnemonic: RIPE** – for technical quality assessment before interpretation  
- **R** – Rotation (spinous process equidistant from clavicle heads)  
- **I** – Inspiration (posterior ribs: 9–10 visible above diaphragm)  
- **P** – Penetration (spine visible behind heart but not too overpenetrated)  
- **E** – Exposure (appropriate field – all lung apices to costophrenic angles)

**Clinical Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **Always compare to previous CXR** – a new opacity in a smoker is cancer until proven otherwise.
2. **A normal CXR does not rule out PE** – next step is CT pulmonary angiogram if intermediate-high clinical probability.
3. **Supine CXRs are treacherous** – pneumothorax may appear as a deep sulcus sign (hyperlucent costophrenic angle) rather than a pleural line; effusions look like diffuse haziness.
4. **The lateral film is your friend** – it confirms the zonal location of a mass and can show retrosternal air (pneumomediastinum).
5. **Nipple shadows** are often bilateral, similar to nodules; look for subcutaneous fat and check if the patient has nipple rings – compare with the other side.
6. **A feeding vessel sign** (a vessel leading directly to a spherical opacity) suggests a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation or septic embolus.
7. **In a post-operative patient**, a crescent of air above the liver on a supine film is pneumoperitoneum – do not confuse with gastric bubble.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Calling a skin fold from an overexposed film a pneumothorax (skin fold has no pleural line and often extends beyond the lung edge).
- Missing a small pneumothorax in a thin patient – look carefully for the pleural line.
- Ignoring the bones – rib metastases or fractures may be the only clue in trauma or malignancy.
- Overcalling cardiomegaly on AP film (magnified) – always repeat PA erect if possible.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team – based on CXR findings:**

| Finding | Action | Threshold |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Tension pneumothorax** (clinical diagnosis + CXR confirms) | Immediate needle decompression; call surgical/medical registrar + alert anaesthetics/ICU. | Any suspicion of tension – do not wait for imaging if clinical instability. |
| **Massive haemothorax** (in trauma – CXR shows opacified hemithorax with mediastinal shift) | Large-bore chest drain (36Fr) + activate massive transfusion protocol; call trauma team leader. | Initial drained >1500 mL of blood or ongoing bleeding >200 mL/h. |
| **Suspected aortic dissection** on CXR (widened mediastinum >8 cm at level of aortic arch, loss of aortic knuckle, tracheal deviation to right, pleural cap) | Urgent CT aortogram; call cardiothoracic registrar and ED consultant. | Blood pressure differential >20 mmHg between arms, history of hypertension. |
| **Severe pulmonary oedema** (bilateral central opacities with pleural effusions, associated

rawMarkdown:
## CXR — Approach

**One-liner:** Systematic interpretation of frontal/lateral chest radiographs; essential for any acute patient; missed findings cause preventable deterioration.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Vignette:** A 68-year-old smoker presents to ED with acute dyspnoea, right-sided pleuritic chest pain, and productive cough of green sputum for three days. Heart rate 110 bpm, temperature 38.2°C, oxygen saturations 89% on room air. Chest exam reveals decreased breath sounds at the right base with bronchial breathing and coarse crackles. Trachea is midline. The junior doctor orders a PA erect CXR.

- **Pleuritic pain + cough + fever + hypoxaemia** → high suspicion of community-acquired pneumonia; CXR will likely show lobar consolidation.
- **Reduced breath sounds and bronchial breathing** → air bronchograms and opacity on the affected lung region.
- **Midline trachea** → no significant mass effect or tension physiology.
- **Smoker + age >65** → increased risk of underlying malignancy; look for additional mass or effusion.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Silent pneumonia** – patient may present with confusion and tachypnoea without cough or fever; CXR shows subtle opacity (especially in elderly).
- **The "normal" early CXR** – in early pneumonia, especially interstitial, the CXR can be normal for 12–24 hours; repeat if strong clinical suspicion.
- **Post-obstructive pneumonia** – from an endobronchial tumour; CXR may show distal consolidation with no air bronchogram and secondary signs like hilar mass or volume loss.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** A **Chest X-ray (CXR) approach** is a structured, systematic method to evaluate the frontal (and when available, lateral) chest radiograph for technical quality, anatomy, and all abnormalities — from lungs and pleura to heart, mediastinum, bones, and soft tissues.

**Why it matters:**  
- CXR is the most frequently requested imaging study — up to 5 million performed annually in the UK alone.  
- An unstructured review misses 20–30% of significant findings (e.g., subtle pneumothorax, small effusion, hilar mass).  
- Junior doctors are expected to interpret CXRs immediately in acute settings; error leads to delayed treatment for conditions like tension pneumothorax, pericardial effusion, or aortic dissection.  
- Common pitfalls include focusing only on the lungs, misidentifying artefacts (e.g., ECG leads, buttons, nipples), and failing to note tube/line positions.  
- The approach must be **reproducible, complete, and fast** — the ABCDE method is standard.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

- **X-ray attenuation:** Four basic densities on a CXR — air (black), fat (grey), soft tissue/water (white), bone (whiter), metal (brightest). The lung appears mostly black because it is ~80% air. Any increase in density (opacity) or decrease (lucency) means pathology.

- **Silhouette sign:** Loss of the normal border between two structures of same radiodensity. Example: a right middle lobe pneumonia effaces the right heart border (because the middle lobe is anterior and adjacent to the heart). A lower lobe pneumonia does not — it silhouettes the diaphragm.

- **Consolidation (air-space opacification):** Alveoli filled with exudate/pus/blood/water → X-rays are absorbed more → white area. **Air bronchogram** — black branching air-filled bronchi visible within the white consolidation — occurs because bronchi remain patent while surrounding alveoli are filled. This is the single most reliable sign of air-space disease (pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, etc.) and essentially rules out atelectasis (where bronchi also collapse).

- **Atelectasis:** Volume loss from alveolar collapse. The lung appears opaque but with **no air bronchogram** (collapsed bronchi); instead, you see displacement of fissures, hila, or mediastinum toward the collapsed side.

- **Pleural effusion:** Fluid in the pleural space layers posteriorly and inferiorly. On an erect PA film, the dependent costophrenic angle blunts initially; larger effusions produce a **meniscus sign** (concave upward border). Supine films cause fluid to layer posteriorly, making the hemithorax appear uniformly hazy.

- **Pneumothorax:** Air in the pleural cavity separates visceral from parietal pleura. The visceral pleura becomes visible as a **thin, white, hairline** (pleural line) with **no lung markings beyond it** (hyperlucent area). In tension, the mediastinum shifts away, and the hemidiaphragm is depressed.

- **Pulmonary oedema:** Interstitial oedema first → Kerley B lines (short horizontal lines at costophrenic angles) and peribronchial cuffing; then alveolar oedema → bilateral central "bat wing" opacities with air bronchograms.

- **Mass:** A tumour (primary or metastatic) appears as a rounded opacity with varying borders. Benign masses often have smooth, well-defined borders; malignant masses may have spiculated borders (corona radiata) or a feeding vessel sign.

- **Mediastinal widening:** May be from aortic dissection (irregular aortic knuckle), lymphadenopathy (bilateral hilar masses – typically sarcoid, TB, lymphoma), or thymoma.

- **Bone lesions:** Rib fractures (especially in trauma) may be subtle on CXR; look for cortical disruption, step-off, or displacement. Osteolytic metastases appear as lucent defects.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**  
- The vignette with consolidation, bronchial breath sounds, and fever is explained by air bronchograms and alveolar filling – pattern of pneumonia.  
- Midline trachea in tension pneumothorax would be a critical shift; absence of shift suggests simple pneumothorax.  
- A silent pneumonia in the elderly: immunosenescence reduces radiographic density, so the opacity may be faint; look for subtle blunting of angle or loss of sharp cardiac border.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Causes | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Infectious** | Community-acquired pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, viruses), hospital-acquired (MRSA, Gram-negatives), tuberculosis, fungal (immunocompromised) | Fever, cough, consolidation with air bronchogram |
| **Cardiogenic** | Acute pulmonary oedema (LVF, fluid overload) | Bilateral central opacity, Kerley B lines, cardiomegaly, effusions |
| **Neoplastic** | Primary lung cancer (SCC, adenocarcinoma), metastasis | Solitary pulmonary nodule (SPN), hilar mass, effusion, no air bronchogram |
| **Traumatic** | Rib fracture, pneumothorax, haemothorax, contusion | History of trauma, rib tenderness, pleural line, opacification from contusion |
| **Autoimmune/Inflammatory** | Sarcoidosis, connective tissue disease (rheumatoid, SLE), hypersensitivity pneumonitis | Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, interstitial pattern, restrictive changes |
| **Idiopathic** | Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), cryptogenic organising pneumonia (COP) | Basal-predominant reticular pattern, honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis |
| **Iatrogenic** | Line misplacement (central line in artery, NG tube in airway), post-procedural pneumothorax, aspiration | Clinical context: after procedure, intubation, or feeding |
| **Vascular** | Pulmonary embolism (rarely visible – only if infarct) | Hampton's hump (wedge-shaped pleural-based opacity), Westermark's sign (oligaemia) – rarely seen |
| **Other** | Foreign body aspiration, atelectasis (post-op, mucus plug), pleural effusion (para-pneumonic, malignant) | Specific history or post-operative state |

**Most common cause overall:** Infection (community-acquired pneumonia) accounts for the vast majority of focal opacities in ambulatory patients. For bilateral diffuse opacities, pulmonary oedema is most common in hospitalised patients.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**The CXR itself is the primary investigation.** The key is a systematic interpretation.

**Systematic ABCDE approach (mnemonic):**

- **A – Airways:** Trace the trachea (midline?), carina (saddle angle <90°?), main bronchi. Look for diver or NG tube misplacement.
- **B – Breathing / Lungs:** Check for:
  - **Pneumothorax** – pleural line, deep sulcus sign (supine), lack of lung markings.
  - **Consolidation** – air bronchogram, opacity, no volume loss.
  - **Atelectasis** – volume loss, no air bronchogram.
  - **Effusion** – blunted costophrenic angle, meniscus.
  - **Diffuse interstitial pattern** – reticular, nodular, or ground-glass.
- **C – Cardiac / Mediastinum:** Heart size (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5 abnormal), mediastinal width (normal <8 cm at the aortic arch), aortic knuckle, hilar position (left hilum usually higher). Check for water bottle heart (pericardial effusion), widened mediastinum (dissection/aneurysm).
- **D – Diaphragm:** Right hemi 1–2 cm higher than left. Flattening suggests hyperinflation (COPD). Look for air under diaphragm (free gas – perforation).
- **E – Everything else:** Bones (ribs, clavicles, spine – fracture/destruction), soft tissues (subcutaneous emphysema), shoulders, upper abdomen (gastric bubble, colonic ileus).

**Additional views/techniques:**
- **Lateral CXR** – clarifies retrocardiac, retrosternal areas, and confirms effusions or masses seen on frontal.
- **Decubitus film** – distinguishes free-flowing (layers) from loculated effusion.
- **Expiration view** – increases sensitivity for small pneumothorax (look for visceral pleural line).
- **CT chest** – gold standard when CXR equivocal for malignancy, PE, ILD, or complex infection.

**Single finding that often confirms:**
- **Air bronchogram** → essentially diagnostic of air-space consolidation (pneumonia/oedema) and excludes atelectasis.
- **Visceral pleural line** without lung markings → confirms pneumothorax.
- **Meniscus sign and blunted CP angle** → confirms pleural effusion of at least 200 mL.

**Rules out (with high sensitivity if negative):**
- A completely normal CXR in a patient with pleuritic pain and dyspnoea makes significant pleural or parenchymal disease unlikely; consider PE or musculoskeletal cause.
- In trauma, a normal CXR does not exclude aortic injury – CT required.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

Management is based on the CXR finding. The table below summarises immediate, ward, and long-term actions.

| Finding | Immediate (first hour) | Ward (24–72h) | Long-term | What NOT to do |
|---------|------------------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Tension pneumothorax** | Immediate needle decompression (14G, 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line or 5th space midaxillary) → large-bore chest drain (28–36Fr, 5th intercostal space midaxillary). | – | Follow-up CXR after drain removal; refer to respiratory if recurrent spontaneous PTx. | Wait for CXR confirmation if clinical suspicion high (distended neck veins, hypotension, absent breath sounds) – decompress first. |
| **Simple pneumothorax** | If >2cm (from pleural line to chest wall at hilum) or symptomatic, insert chest drain (12–14Fr). If <2cm and asymptomatic, observe. | Re-check CXR at 4–6h; if stable size, discharge with advice. | Consider pleurodesis if recurrent. | Do not insert drain for tiny apical pneumothorax <2cm in an asymptomatic patient (risk of conversion to tension). |
| **Large pleural effusion (new)** | Diagnostic thoracentesis (US-guided) – send for LDH, protein, pH, cytology, Gram stain, culture. | If empyema (pH<7.2, glucose<2.2) → chest drain; antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV QDS). | Treat underlying cause: diuretics for heart failure, anti-TB therapy, chemotherapy for malignant. | Do not drain a transudative effusion on appearance alone – check Light's criteria. |
| **Lobar pneumonia with consolidation** | Start antibiotics within 1h: for CAP: amoxicillin 500mg PO TDS (mild) or amoxicillin 1g IV TDS + clarithromycin 500mg IV BD (severe). If healthcare-associated: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV QDS. | Adjust based on cultures; switch to oral when clinically improved. | Check CXR at 6 weeks in smokers or those with risk factors to rule out underlying mass. | Do not give antibiotics for a CXR showing only atelectasis; observe. |
| **Pulmonary oedema (cardiogenic)** | IV furosemide 40–80mg IV (or 5× daily dose if on loop diuretic). Give O2 target 94–98%, consider CPAP if severe. | Optimise heart failure therapy (ACEi, beta-blocker); check echo. | Long-term diuretic dose adjustment, salt restriction, exercise. | Do not fluid bolus (even if hypotensive due to poor LV function); try vasopressor if needed (e.g., dobutamine). |
| **Widened mediastinum (suspected dissection)** | Urgent CT aortogram. BP control: labetalol 20mg IV bolus then infusion 1–2mg/min; target HR 60 bpm and SBP 100–120 mmHg. | If dissection confirmed, surgical or endovascular repair. | Lifelong antihypertensive therapy. | Do not wait for lab results to act; manage immediately. |

**General pitfall:** Never interpret a CXR in isolation from clinical context. Always compare with previous films if available.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

For a **unilateral opacity on CXR**, key differentials: consolidation vs atelectasis vs mass vs effusion.

| Feature | Consolidation (Pneumonia) | Atelectasis | Mass (Tumour) | Pleural Effusion |
|---------|---------------------------|-------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Air bronchogram** | Present | Absent (bronchi also collapse) | Absent (unless secondary pneumonitis) | Absent |
| **Volume loss** | None (or minimal) | Prominent (fissure shift, hilar retraction, mediastinal shift) | None (or if endobronchial, distal atelectasis may be present) | None (but effusion may cause contralateral shift if large) |
| **Borders** | Fleshy, ill-defined, lobar | Concave, sharp at fissures | Well- or ill-defined, rounded | Meniscus sign – concave upward |
| **Silhouette sign** | Yes – e.g., RML pneumonia blurs right heart border | No – atelectasis blurs only if directly adjacent (e.g., LLL atel blurs left hemi-diaphragm) | Usually not – unless it involves adjacent mediastinal structures | May not silhouette unless large; often posterior |
| **Shape** | Lobular or diffuse | Linear, triangular, or wedge | Spherical/oval | Homogeneous, blunting CP angle |
| **Clinical context** | Fever, productive cough, pleuritic pain | Post-operative, pain (mucus plug), shallow breathing | Smoker, weight loss, haemoptysis | Dyspnoea, dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds |
| **Key differentiator** | Air bronchogram + no volume loss = consolidation | Volume loss + no air bronchogram = atelectasis | History + absence of air bronchogram ± spiculated border | Meniscus sign + homogeneous opacity + blunted CP angle |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Missed Finding | How It Presents Late | Key Signs | What To Do |
|----------------|----------------------|-----------|------------|
| **Small pneumothorax missed** | Tension physiology develops (especially on positive pressure ventilation) | Hypotension, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds | Immediate needle decompression → chest drain. |
| **Pneumonia without treatment** | Sepsis, lung abscess, empyema (persistent fever, high WBC, pleural pain) | CXR shows new air-fluid level in consolidation (abscess) or loculated effusion (empyema) | CT chest, drainage of empyema, broaden antibiotics. |
| **Lung cancer missed** | Presentation with SVC obstruction (facial swelling, distended neck veins), haemoptysis, or malignant effusion | Mediastinal widening, hilar mass, or massive effusion | Urgent CT thorax + PET, refer to lung cancer MDT. |
| **Aortic dissection missed** | Rupture – sudden haemodynamic collapse, cardiac tamponade | Widened mediastinum, hypotension, pericardial effusion on CXR | CT aortogram, urgent surgical referral. |
| **Pulmonary oedema missed** | Progressive hypoxia, acute respiratory failure, need for intubation | Bilateral central opacities, PaO2/FiO2 ratio <300 | CPAP, IV diuretics, ICU if NIV fails. |
| **Pericardial effusion (missed on CXR)** | Cardiac tamponade: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins (Beck's triad) | Enlarged cardiac silhouette (water bottle heart) + clear lungs | Urgent echo, pericardiocentesis. |

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: ABCDE**  
- **A** – Airways (trachea, bronchi, tubes)  
- **B** – Breathing (lungs: pneumothorax, consolidation, effusion, atelectasis, interstitial)  
- **C** – Cardiac (heart size, mediastinal width)  
- **D** – Diaphragm (position, free gas)  
- **E** – Everything else (bones, soft tissues, lines)

**Mnemonic: RIPE** – for technical quality assessment before interpretation  
- **R** – Rotation (spinous process equidistant from clavicle heads)  
- **I** – Inspiration (posterior ribs: 9–10 visible above diaphragm)  
- **P** – Penetration (spine visible behind heart but not too overpenetrated)  
- **E** – Exposure (appropriate field – all lung apices to costophrenic angles)

**Clinical Pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **Always compare to previous CXR** – a new opacity in a smoker is cancer until proven otherwise.
2. **A normal CXR does not rule out PE** – next step is CT pulmonary angiogram if intermediate-high clinical probability.
3. **Supine CXRs are treacherous** – pneumothorax may appear as a deep sulcus sign (hyperlucent costophrenic angle) rather than a pleural line; effusions look like diffuse haziness.
4. **The lateral film is your friend** – it confirms the zonal location of a mass and can show retrosternal air (pneumomediastinum).
5. **Nipple shadows** are often bilateral, similar to nodules; look for subcutaneous fat and check if the patient has nipple rings – compare with the other side.
6. **A feeding vessel sign** (a vessel leading directly to a spherical opacity) suggests a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation or septic embolus.
7. **In a post-operative patient**, a crescent of air above the liver on a supine film is pneumoperitoneum – do not confuse with gastric bubble.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Calling a skin fold from an overexposed film a pneumothorax (skin fold has no pleural line and often extends beyond the lung edge).
- Missing a small pneumothorax in a thin patient – look carefully for the pleural line.
- Ignoring the bones – rib metastases or fractures may be the only clue in trauma or malignancy.
- Overcalling cardiomegaly on AP film (magnified) – always repeat PA erect if possible.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team – based on CXR findings:**

| Finding | Action | Threshold |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Tension pneumothorax** (clinical diagnosis + CXR confirms) | Immediate needle decompression; call surgical/medical registrar + alert anaesthetics/ICU. | Any suspicion of tension – do not wait for imaging if clinical instability. |
| **Massive haemothorax** (in trauma – CXR shows opacified hemithorax with mediastinal shift) | Large-bore chest drain (36Fr) + activate massive transfusion protocol; call trauma team leader. | Initial drained >1500 mL of blood or ongoing bleeding >200 mL/h. |
| **Suspected aortic dissection** on CXR (widened mediastinum >8 cm at level of aortic arch, loss of aortic knuckle, tracheal deviation to right, pleural cap) | Urgent CT aortogram; call cardiothoracic registrar and ED consultant. | Blood pressure differential >20 mmHg between arms, history of hypertension. |
| **Severe pulmonary oedema** (bilateral central opacities with pleural effusions, associated
